entry.title: The Four Freedoms and What They Mean for Your Organization
entry.summary: Richard Stallman launched the free software movement in 1983 with an idea that sounded radical at the time: software users deserve freedom. Not just the…
entry.content:
Richard Stallman launched the free software movement in 1983 with an idea that sounded radical at the time: software users deserve freedom. Not just the freedom to run a program, but the freedom to understand it, share it, and improve it. Forty years later, those ideas power much of the internet, and they’re more relevant to nonprofits than most people realize.

The Free Software Foundation defines four freedoms. Here’s what each one looks like when you’re running a community organization instead of a tech company.

Freedom 0: Use the software for any purpose

This sounds obvious, but proprietary software licenses often restrict how you can use what you paid for. Some licenses limit the number of users. Others prohibit certain uses or require separate licenses for different departments. Free software doesn’t care who you are, how big your organization is, or what you do with it. Install it on one computer or fifty. Use it for fundraising, advocacy, education, or mutual aid. No one checks.

For a nonprofit that grows from three staff to thirty, this matters. Your software scales with you at no additional cost.

Freedom 1: Study how the software works

You probably won’t read the source code of LibreOffice or CiviCRM. Most people don’t. But the fact that you can means something. It means there are no hidden features collecting your data. It means security researchers can audit the code and find vulnerabilities before they cause harm. It means the software does what it says it does, and anyone can verify that.

When a proprietary company tells you their software is secure, you have to take their word for it. With free software, you don’t.

Freedom 2: Share copies with others

If your nonprofit finds a tool that works, you can share it with partner organizations, coalition members, or the group down the street doing similar work. You can install it for them. You can help them set it up. No licensing fees, no seat limits, no legal concerns.

This changes the math for entire networks of organizations. One group figures out how to use CiviCRM for donor management, and every allied group benefits. The knowledge and the software both spread freely.

Freedom 3: Modify the software and share your improvements

This is where free software gets interesting for organizations with needs that off-the-shelf tools don’t meet. If your nonprofit needs a feature that doesn’t exist, you (or someone you hire) can build it and contribute it back to the project. Other organizations with the same need benefit from your work, and you benefit from theirs.

The Drupal community, which builds the content management system that runs sites for the ACLU and Doctors Without Borders, works this way. Thousands of organizations contribute improvements that go back into the shared pool. A food shelf in Minneapolis and a human rights organization in Geneva both benefit from code that neither of them wrote alone.

Why this matters now

Software shapes how organizations operate. It determines what data you collect, how you communicate, and who has access to your work. Choosing software that respects your freedom isn’t a technical decision. It’s a decision about values, independence, and the kind of infrastructure you want your mission to depend on.

If you’re curious about where to begin, the Software Guide lists tools organized by the work nonprofits do every day.

entry.title: Your Nonprofit Deserves Better Than "Free"
entry.summary: When someone offers you something for free, it’s worth asking what they get out of it. Gmail costs nothing. Google Docs costs nothing. Facebook and…
entry.content:
When someone offers you something for free, it’s worth asking what they get out of it.

Gmail costs nothing. Google Docs costs nothing. Facebook and Instagram cost nothing. These tools work well, and nonprofits use them everywhere. But they aren’t free in any meaningful sense. You pay with your data, your donors’ data, and your community’s attention. Google reads your email to serve ads. Facebook decides who sees your posts and charges you to reach the people who already follow you. These companies built their business models around extracting value from the people who use their products.

For a nonprofit, this should feel uncomfortable. Organizations that exist to serve their communities shouldn’t have to hand over sensitive information to use basic tools. A domestic violence shelter using Gmail is trusting Google with the email addresses of survivors. A food shelf using a “free” donor management platform is giving a company access to its supporters’ names and giving history. Maybe nothing bad happens with that data. But the point is that you don’t control what happens with it, and you can’t.

The other kind of free

Free and open source software offers a different deal. When the Free Software Foundation talks about “free software,” they mean software that respects your freedom. You can use it however you want. You can look at the code and see what it does. You can share it. You can change it. No one tracks you, locks you in, or mines your data.

This isn’t idealism. It’s practical. When your donor management system runs on CiviCRM, the data stays on your server. When your team writes documents in LibreOffice, nobody analyzes those documents to sell advertising. When your website runs on Drupal or Publii, you own every piece of it.

The cost question

Yes, free and open source software saves money. That matters for organizations running on tight budgets. But the money question can distract from the more important one: who controls your tools?

Proprietary software companies can change their pricing whenever they want. They can discontinue products. They can alter terms of service. If your organization builds its operations around a tool that a company controls, you’re dependent on that company’s decisions. Free software removes that dependency. The code belongs to everyone. If one group stops maintaining it, another can pick it up. Your investment in learning and configuring the tool doesn’t evaporate because a company pivoted to a new market.

Getting started

If this resonates but feels overwhelming, check out the Getting Started page. The short version: pick one tool, try it, and go from there. You don’t need to change everything at once. You just need to start asking better questions about the tools you use and who benefits from your answers.

entry.title: Setting Up Your Site with GitHubPages
entry.summary: Have you ever wanted to put a website on the internet without paying monthly hosting fees? That’s what drew me to Publii and GitHub Pages…
entry.content: Have you ever wanted to put a website on the internet without paying monthly hosting fees? That’s what drew me to Publii and GitHub Pages in the first place. The combination gives you a real website, hosted for free, with no catch.Once you’ve got your GitHub repository ready to go, the next step happens back in Publii. Open up the app and head to your site’s connection settings. This is where you tell Publii to sync everything with the GitHub Pages repository you just created.One thing to know before you get there: Publii won’t build your site properly unless you’ve already added at least one post and picked a theme. If you skipped either of those steps, go back and do that first. It doesn’t need to be a polished post or the perfect theme. Just get something in place so Publii has enough to work with.After that, updating the connection settings takes about two minutes. Point Publii at your repository, hit sync, and your site goes live. No FTP credentials, no server configuration, no terminal commands. You click a button and it’s done.If you’re new to all of this, don’t let the GitHub part intimidate you. The Publii docs walk through every step with screenshots, and once you’ve done it once, syncing future updates feels like saving a file.Give it a try and see your site show up at your GitHub Pages URL. There’s something satisfying about watching it appear.
